decisions.accepted_answer: Private Telegram uses the Telclaude-fronted Hermes private-runtime adapter as the production contract. Telclaude remains the ingress, auth, approval, memory, provider, model-relay, redaction, audit, rollback, and network authority. Hermes runs only behind the Telclaude edge in the contained private runtime; direct Hermes gateway ingress is not a production path. The headless CLI/API-server surfaces are acceptable only as pinned, contained execution mechanisms when their observed feature probes pass, and the cross-turn prepare/approve/execute fallback remains the approval-continuation contract when native mid-run continuation is unavailable.
decisions.cutover_impact: The private workflow may stay in the included cutover set, but production cutover still fails unless execution.cli_headless or another chosen contained private execution mechanism, approval continuation, rollback, network containment, model relay, no-fork proof, and parity fixtures pass from observed evidence.

decisions.accepted_answer: Generated Hermes profiles are produced by the Telclaude profile generator and proven by docs/hermes/profile-generation-proof.json. The proof writes the complete HERMES_HOME artifact set, classifies every output, ties output hashes to the current compatibility lockfile digest, and confirms that model/provider secrets remain relay/vault/edge-owned rather than emitted into the profile.
decisions.cutover_impact: Generated Hermes profiles must be pinned, reproducible, and relay-owned before cutover.
Cron and background workflows cannot cut over until duplicate delivery and resume semantics are proven.
decisions.accepted_answer: Cron and background Hermes workflows use Telclaude-owned workflow-run ledger semantics as the production contract. Every run records workflowRunId, initiating actor, authority actor, trust domain, scope, freshness deadline, budget, idempotency key, approval policy, side-effect ledger reference, cancellation state, retry/backoff state, and rollback-visible status. Duplicate delivery, stale resume, replayed approval, provider timeout, process restart, and cancellation are denied or resumed only from immutable ledger state; Hermes session IDs are routing handles, not authority. Cutover remains blocked until workflow.cron, workflow.longrun, approval.continuation, sideeffect.ledger probes and positive/negative fixtures pass from observed evidence.

decisions.cutover_impact: Social workflows cannot cut over until workspace, memory, network, and identity isolation are proven.
decisions.accepted_answer: Public social identity runs as a separate Telclaude-controlled Hermes trust domain with a dedicated profile, process/container boundary, public/social memory namespace, no private workspace mount, no private/provider scopes, and Telclaude edge custody for social credentials, budgets, approvals, inbound timeline events, and outbound post/reply delivery. Hermes may draft and reason inside that domain, but Telclaude derives actor/profile/domain, applies leakage policy, and executes outbound sends through prepared refs. Cutover remains blocked until edge.social, public.social.isolation, identity.migration, outbound.policy probes and social leakage/budget fixtures pass.

decisions.cutover_impact: Provider-backed workflows cannot cut over until relay-proxy routing and signed write approvals are preserved.
decisions.accepted_answer: Provider powers are exposed to Hermes only through Telclaude-derived MCP/provider tools and sidecar/vault custody. Reads and prepare actions receive wrapper-derived actor, profile/domain, provider account, scope, and audit context; writes execute only from immutable actionRef/outboundRef plus signed approval tokens bound to actor, provider account, service/action, params/body hash, approver, rendered approval/card revision, idempotency key, TTL, and one-time JTI. Direct provider, vault, raw OAuth/API keys, cookies, passwords, MFA state, and provider host egress remain unavailable to Hermes. Cutover remains blocked until provider release-policy, approval-binding, served-MCP provider tools, network denial, and provider-specific fixtures pass.

decisions.cutover_impact: Public-channel workflows cannot cut over until webhook delivery, retries, and identity binding are proven.
decisions.accepted_answer: Production WhatsApp, email, AgentMail, social, and household channel delivery uses the Telclaude edge adapter contract. Telclaude receives raw platform events, authenticates actor/thread, applies allowlist/pairing/roster policy, quarantines attachments, deduplicates by ordering cursor/idempotency key, records audit metadata, and forwards only sanitized InboundEvent refs into the correct Hermes profile/session. Outbound delivery starts as Hermes OutboundRequest, then Telclaude prepares, redacts, rate-limits, approval-gates, executes, retries, dead-letters, and records DeliveryReceipt. Hermes never owns transport credentials or raw session files. Cutover remains blocked until each channel-specific edge probe and positive/negative delivery fixture passes.

The cutover scope is complete parity, not private-only. The included set is the full docs/hermes/cutover-scope.json workflow list: private Telegram, public WhatsApp/email/AgentMail/social identity, household assistant, bank/Clalit/government/Google providers, cron/background long-running workflows, and browser/computer brokered automation. Strict cutover must stay red until every included workflow has resolved decisions, feature-probe evidence, fixture evidence, lockfile pins, network containment, rollback, and no-fork proof.
decisions.cutover_impact: No workflow enters production until the complete included parity set passes; partial/private-only green gates are no longer sufficient.

decisions.accepted_answer: Default model access is ChatGPT/Codex subscription via Hermes openai-codex, with gpt-5.5 as the contained-runtime default when the account live probe allows it, but production custody stays in the Telclaude relay/vault. Contained Hermes receives only relay-scoped endpoint/token material; direct openai-api, OPENAI_API_KEY, or mounted Hermes/Codex OAuth files are fallback or downgrade paths, not the default.
decisions.cutover_impact: Hermes model traffic must route through Telclaude-owned relay boundaries before production use; cutover evidence must prove the subscription-backed default path without exposing raw Codex/OpenAI credentials to contained Hermes.
